input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation uses a multi‑notation MBSE foundation: SysML v1.7 for requirements, functional, and logical architecture, including IBDs; ACME and AADL for component‑connector modeling; MontiArc for structural and behavioral modeling (state machines and MontiArc Automaton). Behavioral simulation is achieved via MathWorks Simulink and other simulation engines. Code generation and execution may employ general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. The framework supports RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT methodologies. Data integration and interpretation rely on specialized software platforms that accept one‑way data streams, as required for active digital twins, and may use databases for contextual data aggregation. These enablers collectively support a comprehensive digital twin architecture.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin’s computational model density follows a multi‑layer MBSE architecture. At the highest abstraction SysML is employed for capturing requirements, functional, logical, and product/technical layers, while architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc represent the component‑and‑connector topology, interfaces, and compositional hierarchy. For detailed behavioral capture, extensions like MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state‑machines are used, and closed‑loop simulation is facilitated through MathWorks Simulink integration and potentially other industry simulation tools. Code generation is supported in general‑purpose languages (C++ and Java). The data flow between models and the physical plant is managed through model‑driven engineering practices, linking requirements to behavior, and aligning model execution with real‑time sensor streams, while ensuring that model relations (e.g., functional→logical→physical mappings) are traceable and versioned.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: Digital Twin development proceeds through multiple MBSE abstraction levels—requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. At the requirements phase, textual and constraint models are captured; the functional architecture is expressed via SysML requirement diagrams and SysML requirement constraints. The logical architecture is captured in SysML interaction blocks or in MontiArc component‑connector models; the product/technical layer is implemented using C++ or Java code and Simulink blocks. Transition between the levels is managed by model synchronization and co‑simulation (e.g., SysML IBDs exported to Simulink for dynamic simulation). Governance across phases requires an overarching MBSE repository and traceability links that tie SysML requirements to C++ code, ensuring that change propagation and version control are maintained via a configuration management system.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twin engineering cycle follows the SMArDT methodology with RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) as a guiding principle. Modeling languages such as SysML, AADL, and MontiArc ADL are used for functional and logical architecture, while MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines capture behavior. The static models are transformed into simulation models in MathWorks Simulink for verification and validation under test conditions defined by engineering teams. Quality assurance is performed through model‑based verification, consistency checks, and simulation trace‑ability. Version control and evolution are managed in a common repository (e.g., Git) where each model artifact and corresponding source code is tagged, and evolution is tracked via change logs that capture the rationale of twin updates. The twin evolves iteratively, with a feedback loop that feeds real‑time data from the physical system into the active components of the twin, thus enabling adaptation and incremental refining of behavior.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
